topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The old oak stood sentinel at the edge of the yard, a towering monument to my childhood enthusiasm. I walked over every Tuesday, a habit ingrained since I was ten, tracing the bark with my fingertips. This week, something was terribly wrong. The space where the massive trunk should have been was just a tangle of freshly disturbed earth and splintered wood. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, heavy pressure spreading through my limbs. I knelt down, brushing away loose soil. The roots, which I remembered painstakingly nurturing for years, were gone. There was no sign of struggle, no evidence of a natural decay. Just a clean, brutal removal. I stood up slowly, my gaze sweeping the perimeter of the property, searching for any misplaced tools or telltale marks. My hands trembled slightly as I touched the raw, exposed roots where the tree had anchored.

[story 2]

I found myself lingering by the empty plot for an hour, the afternoon sun doing little to warm the chill that had settled deep in my core. It was the spot where I’d planted the sapling for my grandmother, a small, hopeful thing then. Now, only stumps remained, jagged scars against the lawn. I circled the area twice, my footsteps crunching on dry leaves, each step feeling heavy with unspoken questions. I kept glancing toward the dense woods bordering the property line, half-expecting to see someone lurking, someone who had done this. My jaw was tight, my breathing shallow. When I finally turned back toward the house, I felt a distinct need to check the security cameras, a frantic urge to verify what my eyes were telling me. The silence of the yard amplified the sound of my own quickened pulse.

[story 3]

It was the sight that stopped me dead in my tracks: the sheer emptiness where the giant had been. I stared at the bare patch of ground, my mouth dry. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my skin prickle. I remember the sheer pride I felt when I first watered that tiny seedling; it was a tangible piece of my own effort. Now, it was just absence. I walked toward the edge of the clearing, my posture stiff, my eyes darting from the cut base to the surrounding fence line. I kept replaying the memory of the tree’s vibrant green canopy, juxtaposed against the stark reality of the destruction. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat—a sharp intake of breath—as I realized the implications of what I was seeing. I didn't move for a long time, just absorbing the stark evidence before forcing myself to move, a reluctant, stiff motion toward the back door.
